data.title: Blackstone approaches $4B acquisition of MacLean Power Systems - report
data.description:
Blackstone (BX) is approaching a more than $4B deal to buy utility-parts maker MacLean Power Systems from Centerbridge Partners, according to a media report on Wednesday.

An announcement on the MacLean takeover may be made as soon as next week, Bloomberg reported, citing people familiar. Talks are in the advanced stage, though they could still be postponed or falter.

According to its website, MacLean supplies hardware used for power transmission and distribution networks, including anchoring and grounding solutions. Centerbridge became the company's majority owner in 2022.

Blackstone (BX) won the contest against other potential buyers of MacLean, including ABB Ltd (OTCPK:ABBNY), which opted not to improve its offer, some of the people told Bloomberg.

Blackstone (BX) did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

data.title: Safe Bulkers signals $124M revenue backlog from Capesize charters while expanding fleet efficiency
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Safe Bulkers, Inc. (SB) Q3 2025

Management View

  • Dr. Loukas Barmparis, President, emphasized the company's ongoing fleet renewal with the sale of two older vessels, stating the "dry bulk market recovered compared to the previous quarter, and we sold 2 of our oldest vessels as part of the company's ongoing fleet renewal strategy." He highlighted the declaration of a $0.05 per share dividend and a strong capital structure, noting "our company maintains a strong capital structure providing flexibility in our capital allocation."
  • Barmparis outlined the order book below 11% of the fleet, with six Phase 3 vessels set for delivery, projecting that by Q1 2027, "Safe Bulkers fleet will be comprised of 35% Phase 3 vessels, 18 out of 51, positioning us favorably to compete based on the fuel efficiency of our vessels."
  • He confirmed a $124 million revenue backlog from Capesize charters, with "all 8 of our Capes...presently period chartered with an average remaining charter duration of almost 1.7 years at an average daily charter rate of $24,800."
  • Barmparis underlined capital resources at $390 million and comfortable leverage of about 35%, reiterating the firm's focus on technological advancement and a "resilient business model with cash flow visibility of $164 million in revenue backlog."
  • Konstantinos Adamopoulos, CFO, stated, "During the third quarter of 2025, we operated in a weaker charter market environment compared to the same period in 2024 with decreased revenue due to lower charter hires and decreased earnings from scrubber fitted vessels."
  • Adamopoulos also highlighted the company’s "adjusted EBITDA for the third quarter of 2025 stood at $36.1 million compared to $41.3 million for the same period in 2024," and "adjusted earnings per share for the third quarter of 2025 was $0.12."

Outlook

  • Management indicated continued fleet renewal with two dual-fuel newbuilds on order for Q1 2027 delivery and expects the dry bulk fleet to grow by about 3% on average in 2025 and 2026.
  • Barmparis discussed an improving freight market rate, supported by "the agreement between U.S. and China with an increasing focus on the existing fleet decarbonization and energy efficient new builds."
  • He forecasted that "freight market has rebounded recently during the start of the third quarter," and expects "growth is expected to come from stronger grain and minor bulk shipments and from longer sailing distances."
  • No explicit numerical forward guidance for EPS or revenue was provided for the upcoming quarter.

Financial Results

  • Adamopoulos reported, "adjusted EBITDA for the third quarter of 2025 stood at $36.1 million compared to $41.3 million for the same period in 2024."
  • Adjusted EPS for the third quarter was $0.12, down from $0.16 in the prior year period.
  • The company operated an average of 46.51 vessels, earning an average time charter equivalent of $15,507, versus 45.27 vessels at $17,108 in the prior year period.
  • Daily vessel earning expenses decreased by 4% to $5,104, while running expenses excluding dry docking and predelivery increased by 1% to $5,060.
  • Cash flow position was $187 million as of November 21, 2025, with $210 million in available undrawn revolving credit facilities, for nearly $400 million in combined liquidity and capital resources.
  • The Board declared a $0.05 dividend per common share, marking the 16th consecutive payout.

Q&A

  • No analyst questions or management Q&A were recorded during this call session, as indicated by the operator and closing remarks from Dr. Barmparis.

Sentiment Analysis

  • The absence of analyst questions suggests neutral to muted sentiment, with no pressing concerns or challenges raised during the session.
  • Management maintained a steady and confident tone, with Barmparis stating, "We have built a resilient business model with cash flow visibility of $164 million in revenue backlog, healthy expansion for a sizable fleet that achieves scale and a meaningful 4.1% annualized dividend yield positioned to leverage on its fuel efficiency."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the tone remains consistent, emphasizing resilience, fleet renewal, and capital strength.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance language remains focused on fleet renewal, technological upgrades, and maintaining strong liquidity, similar to last quarter.
  • There is an increase in contracted revenue backlog visibility from Capesize vessels and an uptick in cash and capital resources.
  • Management confidence is steady, reaffirming dividend continuity and emphasizing a resilient business model.
  • Analysts’ sentiment cannot be compared as no questions were asked this quarter, whereas the previous quarter also lacked substantive analyst engagement.
  • There is continued focus on leverage reduction and advancing fleet efficiency, with new vessel deliveries and ongoing divestment of older tonnage.

Risks and Concerns

  • Barmparis warned of "market fragmentation due to geopolitical reasons for fees and tariffs resulting in increased market volatility."
  • He cited that "trade tensions between the U.S. and China, although truce has been reached, remain a key source of global economic uncertainty."
  • The CFO noted operating in a "weaker charter market environment compared to the same period in 2024 with decreased revenue due to lower charter hires and decreased earnings from scrubber fitted vessels."
  • Management highlighted strong liquidity and capital resources as mitigation strategies to navigate market volatility and support long-term growth.

Final Takeaway

Safe Bulkers, Inc. highlighted a strengthened capital position, improved cash flow visibility through contracted Capesize charters, and continued execution of its fleet renewal strategy. With a focus on technologically advanced vessels and disciplined capital allocation, management underscored their confidence in sustaining shareholder returns and navigating ongoing market volatility, while preparing for future growth with newbuild orders and operational efficiencies.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: CMS unveils price reductions for 15 drugs for 2027
data.description:
Individuals covered under Medicare can expect significant savings on the 15 drugs that were selected earlier this year for drug price negotiations under the Inflation Reduction Act starting in 2027.

While the drugs subject to negotiation were announced  in January, CMS just released the extent of the discounts. 

The drug with the highest percentage reduction from its 2024 list price -- 85% -- is Merck's (MRK) type 2 diabetes medication, Janumet (sitagliptin/metformin), which will now cost $80 for a one-month supply. CMS spent ~$1.1B on the medicine in 2024. 

In 2024, CMS spent the most money -- ~$15.1B -- on Novo Nordisk (NVO) diabetes and weight loss medicines Ozempic, Wegovy, and Rybelsus, which all contain the active ingredient semaglutide. The drugs will now cost $274 a month, down from $959 previously, a discount of about 71%.

Other CMS high-spend drugs that will come down in price in 2027 for a 30-day supply include GSK's (GSK) Trelegy Elipta (fluticasone/umeclidinum/vilanterol) at $175 (-73%); Bristol Myers Squibb's (BMY) Pomalyst (pomalidomide) at $8650 (-60%); AbbVie's (ABBV) Linzess (linaclotide) at $136 (75%); Pfizer's (PFE) Ibrance (palbociclib) at $7871 (-50%); and AstraZeneca's (AZN) Calquence (acalabrutinib) at $8600 (-40%).

data.title: AI already capable of replacing 11.7% of U.S. Workforce, MIT study finds
data.description:
A new study released by the Massachusetts Institute of Technology revealed that artificial intelligence can already replace 11.7% of the U.S. labor market, potentially impacting $1.2 trillion in wages across finance, healthcare, and professional services.

The research, conducted using a labor simulation tool called the Iceberg Index, maps how 151 million U.S. workers across the country interact and are affected by AI advancements.

“Basically, we are creating a digital twin for the U.S. labor market,” said Prasanna Balaprakash, ORNL director and co-leader of the research.

According to the study, the visible effects of AI disruption – the layoffs and role shifts in tech and IT sectors – represent merely 2.2% of total wage exposure, or about $211B.

The Iceberg Index, developed by MIT and Oak Ridge National Laboratory, reveals a much deeper impact lurking beneath the surface, affecting routine functions in human resources, logistics, finance, and office administration that are often overlooked in automation forecasts. The index treats workers as individual agents, mapping more than 32,000 skills across 923 occupations in 3,000 counties.

Several state governments have partnered with the researchers to validate the model and explore potential policy responses, according to the study. Tennessee has already cited the Iceberg Index in its official AI Workforce Action Plan, while Utah and North Carolina are preparing similar reports based on the tool’s modeling capabilities.

The research challenges common assumptions about AI risk, demonstrating that exposure extends beyond tech hubs to all 50 states, including inland and rural regions.

According to the report, “Project Iceberg enables policymakers and business leaders to identify exposure hotspots, prioritize training and infrastructure investments, and test interventions before committing billions to implementation.”

This capability allows officials to test different policy approaches before committing significant resources to workforce development initiatives.

Researchers emphasize that the Iceberg Index is not predicting exactly when or where jobs will be lost but rather providing a skills-centered snapshot of what current AI systems can already accomplish.

Balaprakash noted that many of Tennessee’s core sectors still depend heavily on physical work, offering some protection from purely digital automation.

“It is really aimed towards getting in and starting to try out different scenarios,” North Carolina state Senator DeAndrea Salvador, who has worked closely with MIT on the project, said of the tool, which is positioned as a sandbox that states can use to prepare for AI’s growing impact on their workforces.

More on the labor market

  • U.S. stocks close in the red as labor market fear dominate, traders flock to bonds
  • Cleveland Fed Pres. Hammack sees inflation as ongoing concern despite labor market softening
  • Chicago Fed's Goolsbee is 'uneasy' about inflation, says labor market doesn't signal recession
  • ‘Revisions are the story of the year in the labor market’ – Jefferies’s Zervos
  • Nonfarm payrolls surprise to the upside in September as unemployment rate ticks up

data.title: Wall Street asks, is the worst finally behind Abercrombie & Fitch after a solid Q3?
data.description:
Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF) posted its twelfth consecutive quarter of revenue growth, 15% comparable sales at Hollister, and 12% operating margin, fueling a ~40% rally in the stock on Tuesday and giving investors hope that the worst is behind the retailer.

The year got off to a rough start for ANF as tariff pressures and warnings about discretionary spending among weary consumers crushed ANF shares, driving the stock down 60% in just the first quarter of 2025.

With the help of Hollister’s enduring popularity with teens and Gen Z, trade deals with Vietnam and Cambodia, and resilient consumer spending, Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF) made a measured comeback with shares recouping nearly half of its Q1 drop by August before being chased lower again during in the third quarter on cautious guidance on remaining tariff costs (20% with Vietnam, 19% with Cambodia).

Fortunately, Q3 might be the inflection point that investors were waiting for.

“We think the stock’s reaction [Tuesday] might be evidence of the Street giving credence to the improvements the management team has made and eyes shifting to the opportunity in ’26,” said Jefferies analyst Corey Tarlowe.

“We think this momentum can be chased,” Tarlowe added, “given investors can look to ’26 and see easy ANF compares, a Hollister that is still strong, and a margin profile that is improving as the tariff narrative is derisked.”

Agrees UBS’s Mauricio Serna, who thinks that while the post-earnings rally was positioning, he remains bullish on ANF’s outlook on Hollister’s “robust” sales momentum, Abercrombie’s ongoing sales recovery, and tariff headwinds moderating after Q4.

Morgan Stanley’s Alex Straton also sees green shoots pertaining to ANF’s financial profile but cautions that there are still speed bumps ahead. 

“We exit the print more constructive as ANF’s growth & margin profile continues to prove more durable/better than we thought.”

However, Abercrombie is is not completely out of the woods yet, Straton argues as there is still the holiday season to navigate. While industry forecasts look for a strong 2025 season, consumers are still bargain-hunting to offset inflationary pressures elsewhere, leaving specialty retailers like ANF vulnerable.

“We suspect the bear thesis remains intact – that Hollister sales momentum will slow, ANF topline will remain under pressure, and the business will subsequently suffer severe growth/profitability erosion back towards industry norms over time,” Straton writes, maintaining her Equal Weight rating but with a higher price target of $95.

Margin pressure also keeps Citi Research analyst Paul Lejuez Neutral on Abercrombie. Given the retailer's limited pricing power due to its teen positioning, ANF will "bear much of the margin burden of higher tariffs, limiting EPS upside." 

Abercrombie & Fitch (ANF) shares are up for a second day, bringing the two day total to +45%.

data.title: Google selling 500,000 chips could boost earnings by 3%, MS says
data.description:
Amid reports that Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) is in discussions to sell its tensor processing units to Meta (META), Morgan Stanley believes it could be a moderate boost to the tech giant's sales and earnings.

“The extent to which GOOGL sells TPUs through a [first-party] model can indeed move the needle, as our sensitivity work shows that every ~500k chips they sell externally could represent $13bn (~11%) upside to our above Street '27 Google Cloud Revenue estimates and ~3% ($.37) upside to '27 GOOGL [earnings per share],” Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Brian Nowak, wrote in a note to clients.

If Google's cloud growth were to accelerate and the company could see an expansion into the semiconductor market, that would “likely lead to (or support) a high multiple on GOOGL (as we have observed in [the] past couple months),” the analysts explained.

The analysts added that a sales forecast of 500,000 to 1M for Google may not be “unreasonable,” given that Nvidia (NVDA) is expected to ship around 8M GPUs in 2027, assuming the capacity is there. 

Delving deeper, the analysts said the strategy around Google's external push for its TPUs is still a big unknown, with three key questions for investors: its business model; pricing; and the workloads the TPUs are capable of handling. 

There's also the implications for how Google's external TPU sales push impacts semiconductor companies. According to the analysts, the belief is that it will benefit Broadcom (AVGO), which co-designs Google's TPUs and have little, if any impact on Nvidia and AMD (AMD).

“As far as Google’s success on the LLM side, we think Nvidia has gained share at Google this year,” the analysts added. “With Google spending about $20bn or so on Nvidia vs. spending in the teens on TPU. That will shift back next year to some degree, but we don’t expect growth to be winner-take-all. For now, we don’t think having Gemini overtake GPT-5 as the leading model means competitors will slow down, as we’ve heard time and time again from leaders in the tech industry how important this race is, and we expect it to remain competitive — especially as scaling laws seem to be intact.”

data.title: NetApp delivers strong Q2, but analysts remain uncertain on fiscal 2027
data.description:
NetApp (NTAP) delivered stronger-than-expected second-quarter fiscal 2026 results, but analysts note uncertainty heading into fiscal 2027 due to the memory supercycle.

Shares were down 2% by noon trading on Wednesday.

"NTAP posted a better-than-feared F2Q26 beat with revenue of $1.71B (+3% Y/Y or +4% ex-Spot) and record gross margins, supported by strength in all-flash/Public Cloud, and outperformance in Product and Public Cloud gross margins — primarily a function of mix," said Morgan Stanley analysts, led by Erik Woodring, in an investor note.

Morgan Stanley retained its Equal-weight rating and $117 price target on the stock.

"We believe it's inevitable NTAP faces headwinds from higher memory prices/the risk of supply shortages beyond FY26 (ending April '26) — but the question is, how much? History tells us Storage vendors were able to pass off higher input costs in the 2017 cycle, but today's environment feels more challenging, and more risky," Woodring added.

Bank of America analysts echoed these memory concerns.

"Given pre-buys, we do not expect a negative impact from NAND price on product GM in F26 and model margins staying in the high 50% range for F3Q/F4Q," said BofA analysts, led by Wamsi Mohan, in a note. "However, NTAP may need to raise prices if NAND prices sustain at high levels, and that could lead to some demand destruction, in our opinion. We model product GM re-rating lower to mid-50% in F27."

BofA reiterated its Neutral rating and nudged up its price target to $129 from $125.

NetApp competitor Pure Storage (PSTG) had increased 3.7% during Wednesday trading while Nutanix (NTNX) had plunged 18%.

data.title: Lee Enterprises outlines path to $450M digital revenue by 2030 amid digital margin expansion and $50M rights offering
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Lee Enterprises (LEE) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Kevin Mowbray, President and CEO, opened by stating the company finished fiscal 2025 with $562 million in total revenue, with 53% from digital, emphasizing "the strong foundation of Lee's future as a digital-first company." He highlighted $94 million in digital-only subscription revenue from 633,000 subscribers and industry-leading 16% year-over-year digital subscription revenue growth, despite earlier severance impacts.
  • Mowbray underscored Amplified Digital Agency's performance, surpassing $100 million in revenue for the year with 5% same-store growth, calling it "industry-leading" and a sign of confidence in sustainable growth.
  • He stated, "Our 3-pillar digital growth strategy is expected to result in $450 million in digital revenue by 2030," and reaffirmed the company's commitment to digital transformation and cost management.
  • COO Nathan Bekke noted, "digital-only subscription revenue increased 16% on a same-store basis," and growing digital advertising through AI-powered tools and SMB retention led to $103 million in Amplified revenue. He emphasized the company's transition, saying, "digital represents 53% of total revenue, meaning we have already surpassed the critical revenue inflection point where digital leads the enterprise."
  • CFO Tim Millage highlighted more than 12% annual digital revenue growth from fiscal 2021 to 2025 and discussed the execution of $40 million in annualized cost reductions in Q2, with an additional $10 million entering fiscal 2026, leading to a year-end cash cost of $524 million. Millage reported, "we have reduced debt, $121 million since [2020]," and described strategic pension plan termination and upcoming asset monetizations.
  • Mowbray announced a $50 million common stock rights offering to support digital transformation and deleveraging, with expected $18 million annual interest savings if completed, and acknowledged the upcoming departure of CFO Millage in early 2026.

Outlook

  • Mowbray reiterated that "the key driver of our digital revenue growth is our digital-only subscription revenue, not only for 2026, but for the next 5 years," projecting $175 million in recurring digital subscription revenue and 1.2 million digital subscribers by fiscal 2030.
  • The company aims for over $250 million in digital advertising revenue by 2030, with total digital revenue targeted at $450 million. For fiscal 2026, management expects adjusted EBITDA growth in the mid-single digits.
  • Amplified Digital Agency and owned digital properties are described as the main engines for advertising growth, with continued investments in data-driven ad tech and branded content.
  • Mowbray set expectations for "an update on our next quarter's call on the recapitalization investment efforts that are underway." No changes to the long-term digital transition strategy were announced compared to prior quarters, but the rights offering and interest rate reduction add new financial levers.

Financial Results

  • Fiscal 2025 total revenue was $562 million, with digital revenue at $298 million, representing 53% of the total. Digital-only subscription revenue reached $94 million from 633,000 subscribers, and Amplified Digital Agency contributed $103 million out of $184 million in digital advertising revenue.
  • Cost reductions totaling $50 million were executed, bringing cash costs to $524 million for the year, a 5% decrease. Asset sales closed at $9 million in 2025, with $25 million more identified for future monetization.
  • Adjusted EBITDA grew for the second consecutive quarter when adjusted for the extra week in the prior year, though the exact figure was not disclosed. Debt was reduced by roughly $3.5 million in the fiscal year, excluding increases from waivers related to the February cyber incident.

Q&A

  • Jared Marks asked, "What was the total debt reduction in the fourth fiscal quarter and the full fiscal year." Tim Millage answered, "since our credit agreement was launched in 2020, we have reduced debt, $121 million since that time. In 2025...our debt was reduced roughly $3.5 million in the fiscal year as a result of the operations and asset sales."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts' sentiment was neutral, focusing on factual debt reduction figures without pressing on broader strategic risks or guidance.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks, frequently using terms like "confident," "encouraged," and "on track" when discussing digital transformation and future revenue targets. In the Q&A, responses remained factual and measured.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, the management tone was similarly optimistic, but this quarter included more emphasis on long-term digital targets, cost controls, and capital structure updates. Analyst tone remained neutral quarter-over-quarter with limited probing.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reaffirmed its digital-first strategy and long-term targets, with digital revenue now making up 53% of total revenue versus 55% cited in the prior quarter. The $450 million digital revenue target was extended to 2030 from 2028 in the previous quarter.
  • Amplified Digital Agency growth moderated from 10% in Q3 to 5% in Q4 on a same-store basis. Total digital subscribers declined from 670,000 to 633,000, referencing prior quarter declines linked to a cyber incident.
  • Management's confidence in digital transformation and cost management remained high, while the new $50 million rights offering and associated interest savings represent a material financial development not present in Q3. Announced CFO transition adds a new leadership change.
  • Analyst focus shifted from user engagement metrics and digital product strategy in Q3 to debt reduction and balance sheet topics in Q4.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management referenced the February cyber incident as having "interrupted efforts on several key projects in 2025," but expressed confidence in recovery plans for FY 2026.
  • The company continues to face competitive pressures in digital advertising but views AI-powered innovation and local focus as mitigating factors.
  • Asset monetization and execution of the rights offering were identified as critical to liquidity and debt reduction, with management stating the importance of "successful completion" for interest savings.
  • The upcoming CFO transition may introduce operational risk, though management indicated a succession plan update will follow on the next call.

Final Takeaway

Lee Enterprises closed fiscal 2025 emphasizing its successful digital transformation, with digital revenue now surpassing half of total revenue and clear targets set for continued expansion through 2030. Management highlighted robust growth in digital subscriptions and advertising, disciplined cost reductions, and new capital initiatives including a $50 million rights offering and planned debt interest savings. The company remains focused on executing its digital-first strategy, optimizing its portfolio, and delivering long-term value to shareholders while navigating leadership changes and near-term operational challenges linked to earlier disruptions.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
